---
title: "Export widgets to static HTML"
---

A saved widget [document](save-restore.qmd) can be rendered to a **self-contained HTML
page** — no running kernel. cositos reuses the stock ipywidgets embed format: the state is
embedded as a `widget-state+json` block and the frontend (anywidget's `AnyModel`) loads
from a CDN.

## A live widget, rendered statically

The button below is a real cositos widget. Its state was serialized in Python; the
frontend ESM runs in your browser against that embedded state. There is **no kernel** —
interaction is client-side.

```{python}
# | output: asis
from IPython.display import HTML

from cositos import dump_document, embed_snippet

ESM = """
export default {
  render({ model, el }) {
    const btn = document.createElement("button");
    const out = document.createElement("span");
    const paint = () => { out.textContent = " count = " + model.get("n"); };
    btn.textContent = "increment";
    btn.addEventListener("click", () => { model.set("n", model.get("n") + 1); model.save_changes(); });
    model.on("change:n", paint);
    paint();
    el.appendChild(btn); el.appendChild(out);
  }
}
"""

doc = dump_document([("counter", {"_esm": ESM, "n": 5})])
HTML(embed_snippet(doc))
```

## Writing a standalone file

`embed_html` returns a complete page; `write_html` writes it to disk:

```python
from cositos import dump_document, write_html

doc = dump_document([("counter", {"_esm": ESM, "n": 0})])
write_html("counter.html", doc)   # open in any browser (needs internet for the CDN)
```

- **`embed_snippet(doc)`** — just the loader + state + view scripts, to drop into an
  existing page (this is what the widget above uses, and what Quarto embeds).
- **`embed_html(doc)`** — a full standalone HTML document.

The same mechanism underlies the Quarto/JupyterBook/nbconvert recipes in the repo's
`examples/static-export/`.

## Composing widgets (references)

A plain cositos widget is emitted as anywidget's `AnyModel`. `AnyModel` declares no
*widget-reference* traits, so an `"IPY_MODEL_<id>"` string in its state (for example a
`children` list) reaches the frontend as a **literal string, not a resolved child
model** — reference-based composition does **not** work against plain anywidget widgets.

Composition works when the container is a real ipywidgets **controls** model, because
those declare their reference traits with `widget_serialization` (the `unpack_models`
deserializer). Emit a `VBoxModel` (`@jupyter-widgets/controls`) plus a companion
`LayoutModel` (`@jupyter-widgets/base`) — both at module version `2.0.0`, **not**
anywidget's `~0.11.*` — whose `children` reference the anywidget children:

```python
from examples.composition.build import build_html  # runnable, browser-verified
open("vbox.html", "w").write(build_html())          # VBox renders both children
```

See `examples/composition/build.py` for the full recipe.

### Limitation: `jslink` does not work in static export

`jslink`/`dlink` create a `LinkModel` that the html-manager only instantiates when a view
references it. In a backend-less static export the `LinkModel` is viewless and
unreferenced, so it is never created and **link updates do not propagate**. Linking
widgets requires a live kernel.

## Next steps

- [Save & restore](save-restore.qmd) — persist and reload widget state as JSON.
- [State discipline](state-discipline.qmd) — compose many widgets through one model
  (the right way to build an exportable multi-widget UI).
- [Widget gallery](widgets-gallery.qmd) — more ready-to-embed widgets.
